basics.title: How to Read a Recipe
basics.intro: Two minutes of reading saves a ruined supper. Before anything touches a pan:
steps: Read the whole recipe once, start to finish.
Check you have every ingredient — the grocery list is your friend.
Set out the pans and tools it mentions.
Note the times: when it says 'preheat', that's your starting gun.
basics.tip: Recipes here always list ingredients in the order you'll use them.

basics.title: Measuring: Cups, Sticks, and Spoons
basics.intro: American recipes measure by volume. The ones worth knowing by heart:
points: Flour: spoon it into the cup and level it off — don't scoop or pack it.
basics.tip: Dry measuring cups get leveled with a knife; liquid cups get read at eye level.

basics.title: Easy Substitutions
basics.intro: Out of something? Before driving to Publix, try these:
points: No buttermilk: 1 cup milk + 1 tablespoon vinegar, wait 5 minutes.
No baking powder: 1/4 teaspoon baking soda + 1/2 teaspoon vinegar per teaspoon needed.
No fresh garlic: 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder per clove.
No broth: water plus a little extra butter and salt gets you close.
No sour cream: plain yogurt, one for one.
basics.tip: Write the substitution on a sticky note in the cookbook so you remember it worked.

basics.title: Keeping Food Safe
basics.intro: The rules Mamaw already knows, written down anyway:
points: Chicken is done at 165°F — when in doubt, use the thermometer.
Leftovers go in the refrigerator within 2 hours of cooking.
Most leftovers keep 3 days in the refrigerator; label the container with the day.
When reheating, make it steaming hot all the way through.
If it smells wrong, it is wrong. Out it goes.
basics.tip: A roll of masking tape and a marker by the refrigerator ends the mystery-container problem.
